trade.summary: Russia-Turkey trade surged dramatically after 2022, as Turkey refused to join Western sanctions and became a crucial hub for Russia to access global markets. Russia exports natural gas (TurkStream), crude oil, metals, grain, and fertilizers to Turkey. Turkey exports food products, textiles, machinery, and consumer goods to Russia. Turkey has also become a re-export hub for Western goods entering Russia, which has attracted Western scrutiny. This is one of the most important bilateral trade relationships for Russia's sanctions-era economy.
disputes: Western pressure on Turkey to restrict Russian sanctions evasion through Turkish territory
Disputes over grain corridor negotiation roles

diplomatic.status: complex — Turkey maintains strategic autonomy, acting as intermediary
diplomatic.summary: Russia-Turkey relations are among the most complex in current geopolitics. Turkey is a NATO member that refused to sanction Russia, providing Moscow with critical economic lifelines. President Erdogan cultivates personal rapport with Putin and has mediated the Black Sea Grain Initiative and prisoner exchanges. Yet the two countries are on opposing sides in Syria, Libya, and Nagorno-Karabakh. Turkey controls Bosphorus/Dardanelles straits and has invoked Montreux Convention to restrict Russian warship transit since February 2022. The relationship is simultaneously adversarial, complementary, and transactional.
recent_incidents: Turkey mediated the Black Sea Grain Initiative (2022-2023, now collapsed)
Turkey closed Bosphorus to Russian warships citing Montreux Convention (2022)
Turkey-Russia negotiations on Akkuyu nuclear plant (Rosatom project)
Turkey hosted diplomatic contacts between Ukraine and Russia
Turkish drones (Bayraktar) sold to Ukraine, used against Russian forces — significant irritant

military.summary: Russia and Turkey have competing military presences in Syria (Russia supports Assad, Turkey supports opposition and maintains forces in northern Syria), Libya (supported opposing factions), and competed in Nagorno-Karabakh (Armenia vs Azerbaijan). Turkey shot down a Russian Su-24 aircraft in 2015. Despite these conflicts, both pragmatically manage military deconfliction. Turkey sold Bayraktar drones to Ukraine, which were effective against Russian forces. Russia operates Akkuyu nuclear plant construction in Turkey. Turkey maintains NATO membership while purchasing Russian S-400 air defense systems.
military.alliance_status: neither allied nor openly hostile — complex strategic competition with functional coordination
incidents: Turkey shot down Russian Su-24 (2015) — major crisis, resolved 2016
Russian Bayraktar drone victims in Ukraine created irritant
Competing military positions in Syria remain unresolved

financial.summary: Turkey has become a critical financial intermediary for Russia. Russian oligarchs have relocated assets to Turkey. Turkish banks (with some exceptions due to secondary sanctions risk) have maintained correspondent banking with Russia. Mir payment card system (Russia's domestic alternative to Visa/Mastercard) has been adopted in Turkey, though major Turkish banks later restricted it under US pressure. Russian investment in Turkish real estate is significant.

energy.summary: Energy is the most strategically critical dimension. Turkey is the largest customer for Russian natural gas via TurkStream and historic pipelines. Russia supplies approximately 40-45% of Turkey's natural gas needs. Turkey has used its position as a gas transit hub (and Akkuyu nuclear project) as leverage in the broader relationship. Turkey has also discussed becoming a regional gas hub to re-sell Russian gas to Europe, a strategically significant proposal.

scientific.summary: Scientific collaboration exists through engineering, energy technology, and agricultural science. The Akkuyu nuclear project involves significant Russian-Turkish technical cooperation (Rosatom operating the plant, Turkish engineers being trained). Academic exchanges occur, and Turkey is relatively open to Russian researchers unlike EU states.
